mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic services in a Kubernetes environment, leveraging the operator pattern for simplified management. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install ECK.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for ECK to manage Elastic resources. This step installs them from the ECK Helm chart.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the required CRDs for ECK.

steps.title: Install ECK Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace using Helm.

```bash

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --create-namespace --version 2.14.0

```

> Installs the ECK operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK Operator installation
steps.description:
After installation, it's important to verify that the ECK operator is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

kubectl get crd

```

> Check that the ECK operator pod is running.
> Verify that the CRDs have been installed.

steps.title: Create an ElasticSearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a Custom Resource (CR) definition.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.15.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Creates a simple Elasticsearch cluster with 3 nodes.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.15.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
Ensure that the Elasticsearch cluster is up and running.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch

```

> Check the status of the Elasticsearch cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall ECK Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this will also delete any managed Elasticsearch clusters, leading to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade ECK Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your Elasticsearch data. This step will upgrade the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -o json > elasticsearch-backup.json

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.1

kubectl get pods --namespace elastic-system

```

> Backup existing Elasticsearch configurations.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the ECK operator pod is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The CRDs may not be compatible with the current Kubernetes version.

**Fix:**
Ensure you are using a compatible version of Kubernetes (>=1.26).

**Affected versions:** 1.26+

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Elasticsearch cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Elasticsearch pods may be in a crash loop or not starting correctly.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Elasticsearch pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Elasticsearch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service configurations may be blocking access.

**Fix:**
Verify network policies and ensure the service is correctly configured.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources available.

**Fix:**
Check node resource availability and adjust resource requests/limits in the Elasticsearch CR.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.12 or 2.13, ensure to update your Elasticsearch CRs to comply with the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'Green' when running 'kubectl get elasticsearch'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.15.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the minimum resource requirements.
